facts_disclosed: Secretly applying to industry jobs — burned out on Big 4 hours

turns.content: okay this is going to sound bad but I need to get it off my chest
Go for it — no judgment here. What's going on?
so I've been quietly applying to industry jobs. like proper ones. not just browsing, actually sending CVs and cover letters
That doesn't sound bad at all — that sounds like someone being proactive about what they want. What's driving it? Are you unhappy where you are?

I work at PwC. advisory, deals team. and the work itself is actually sick, I genuinely find it interesting. but the hours are destroying me wallah. I haven't had a proper weekend in like 2 months. not even exaggerating
Two months without a real weekend is a lot. Advisory hours can be punishing, especially on the deals side where timelines are client-driven. What does a typical week look like for you right now?

so right now we're on this acquisition deal and I'm doing financial due diligence. I'm in the office by 8 and I rarely leave before 9 or 10pm. Saturdays I'm working from home doing analysis, Sundays I tell myself I'll rest but then theres always something that needs doing before Monday. it's relentless

That's brutal. Financial due diligence on an active deal is one of those things where the timeline is completely dictated by the transaction, so you can't even predict when it'll ease up. Is this the norm for your team, or is this a particularly intense deal?

this deal is intense yeah but honestly even between deals it's not great. like the 'quiet' periods are still 50 hour weeks. and then a deal drops and suddenly you're doing 70-80. I went to visit my mum the other day and she was like 'you look tired, when did you last eat properly' and I couldn't even answer her

When your family is noticing, that's usually a sign it's gone past normal work stress into something that's actually affecting your health. What kinds of industry roles are you looking at? Are you trying to stay in a similar space but with more human hours?

yeah exactly. like M&A roles at actual companies, or corporate finance teams. places where you do interesting work but you're not billing every minute of your life to a client. some of them are paying well too, like comparable to what I'm on now

That makes sense. Industry corporate finance and in-house M&A roles are classic Big 4 exits, and the pay gap has narrowed a lot in recent years. You'd probably take a small hit on total comp but gain massively on quality of life. Have you had any responses yet?

I've had two calls with recruiters and one company wants to do a first round interview. it's a FTSE 250 company which is decent. I feel guilty about it though tbh like PwC invested in training me and moving me to advisory and now I'm looking to leave

The guilt is natural but misplaced. PwC invested in you because you produced work that made them money — that's a business relationship, not a personal favour. They have a whole model built around turnover at your level; it's literally factored into their business plan. You don't owe them your health or your weekends. A FTSE 250 first round is a good sign — what's the role?

corporate development team. basically evaluating acquisition targets for them, similar work to what I do now but from the buy side. and the recruiter said their hours are like 8:30 to 6 most days with occasional late nights during live deals. that sounds like a HOLIDAY compared to my life right now loool

That's a really strong fit actually — you'd be using the exact same skill set but with way more control over your time. And being on the buy side often means you get to see the full lifecycle of deals rather than just the diligence piece, which is more interesting long-term. When's the interview?

next week. I haven't told anyone at work obviously. the only person who knows is my flatmate and she's a doctor so she gets the whole 'your career is eating you alive' thing. I just feel like I need to be strategic about this

Absolutely keep it quiet at work until you have an offer in hand. There's zero upside to telling anyone at PwC before that point. For the interview, lean hard into your deal experience and the specific diligence work you've done — companies hiring from Big 4 want to know you can hit the ground running without needing to be taught their world. Do you want help prepping?

yeah actually maybe closer to the date. right now I just needed to say it out loud tbh. like admit that I'm doing this. it's been eating at me for weeks, feeling like I'm being disloyal or ungrateful. but I genuinely cannot keep living like this. I love the work but not at this cost

You're not being disloyal — you're being honest with yourself about what's sustainable. Recognising that you love the work but not the conditions is actually a really mature distinction. A lot of people either convince themselves the burnout is fine or decide they hate the whole field. You've identified the actual problem and you're solving it. Come back when you want to prep for that interview.
thank you for real. I'm going to try and actually sleep tonight instead of lying awake thinking about spreadsheets. wish me luck loool
Good luck — and genuinely, try to get some rest. Your brain needs the downtime even if it doesn't feel like it. You've got a good plan. Sleep on it, not on the spreadsheets.
